content: Setting up a proc-macro crate in Rust is fundamentally different from doing code generation in languages like Python or JavaScript because you are not manipulating strings; you are manipulating the Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) directly. When you write a derive macro, your compiler passes the AST of the input item to your macro function via the DeriveInput struct defined by syn, which means you have access to concrete tokens and nodes rather than parsing text. The idiomatic Rust approach involves creating a proc-macro crate that exposes an extern crate attribute or a module-level export for this derive macro, allowing users to add #[my_derive_macro] above their structs. Inside your main.rs file, you must import syn and quote first because these crates provide the foundational data structures and macros needed to manipulate code. The core of your implementation is the function that receives DeriveInput as an argument; this struct contains fields like ident for the name, vis for visibility modifiers, tokens for attributes, and impl_generics and where_clauses which hold the generic parameters and trait bounds respectively. You parse this input by iterating over tokens to extract the type name and its associated generics, then you can use quote! macro to build a String representation of the code you intend to generate. When using quote!, remember that it expands at compile time into a string literal that will be inserted back into the source file, so if you want to output an impl block for a struct with generic parameters T and U, your generated code should look like impl<T: Trait<U>, U> MyTrait for StructName { ... }. It is critical to handle errors explicitly because proc-macros are part of the compilation process; failing to parse a DeriveInput or encountering invalid syntax will halt compilation unless you return an Option<syn::Result>. You can implement this by wrapping your token parsing in a Result<T, syn::Error> and then using syn::Error::new() with specific diagnostic messages when something goes wrong. This error handling ensures that developers get clear feedback on why their derive macro failed rather than a cryptic panic or runtime crash. Finally, you register your derived macro with the compiler by adding #[proc_macro_derive(MyMacro)] to your module declaration in the proc-macro crate's lib.rs file; this tells the Rust compiler which function within your crate will be invoked whenever it encounters the MyMacro attribute on other crates. This registration step is what makes your custom derive macro usable by external libraries, completing the setup from parsing input through code generation to final integration with the compiler pipeline.
